functions.doc: Convert any value to its string representation (Python `str`).

operators.doc: Less-than comparison: `true` when the left operand is strictly less than the right.

examples.doc:
`<`/`lt` returns `true` when the current context value is strictly less than the
given value.
examples.name: OperatorLessThan

examples.doc:
`<=`/`le` returns `true` when the current context value is less than or equal to the
given value.
examples.name: OperatorLessOrEqual

operators.doc: Equality comparison: `true` when both operands are equal.

examples.doc: `==`/`eq` returns `true` when both operands are equal.
examples.name: OperatorEqual

operators.doc: Inequality comparison: `true` when the operands differ.

examples.doc: `!=`/`ne` returns `true` when the operands differ.
examples.name: OperatorNotEqual

examples.doc:
`>=`/`ge` returns `true` when the current context value is greater than or equal to
the given value.
examples.name: OperatorGreaterOrEqual

examples.doc:
`>`/`gt` returns `true` when the current context value is strictly greater than the
given value.
examples.name: OperatorGreaterThan

operators.doc: Addition for numbers, concatenation for strings.

examples.doc:
Adds 100 to input integer and converts result into string.
Illustrates usage of `call` attribute `value`.
examples.name: ConvertValue

examples.doc:
Adds suffix to input string.
Illustrates functionality of `expr` rule in form of monad.
examples.name: ExprSimpleMonads1

examples.doc: `-`/`sub` subtracts the given value from the current context value.
examples.name: OperatorSubtract

examples.doc: `*`/`mul` multiplies the current context value by the given value.
examples.name: OperatorMultiply

operators.doc: True division; the result is a float.

examples.doc: `/`/`div` performs true division; the result is a float.
examples.name: OperatorDivide

operators.doc: Modulo: the remainder of a division.

examples.doc:
`%`/`mod` returns the remainder of dividing the current context value by the given
value.
examples.name: OperatorModulo

operators.doc: Logical conjunction by truthiness (Python `and`). Returns one of the operands, not a strict boolean.

examples.doc:
`and`/`&&` use Python logical conjunction (truthiness), not bitwise `&`.
With integers, `6 and 3` yields `3`, not `2`.
examples.name: ExprLogicalAnd

operators.doc: Logical disjunction by truthiness (Python `or`). Returns the first truthy operand and treats `NO_CONTENT` as falsy.

examples.doc:
`or`/`||` use Python logical disjunction (truthiness), not bitwise `|`.
Returns the first truthy operand or the last operand.
examples.name: ExprLogicalOr

examples.doc: `or` treats `NO_CONTENT` as falsy, so a missing value falls through to the fallback.

rules.name: value

rules.description:
Stores `context.this` under the given name in the *current* context scope.
Returns `context.this` unchanged (pass-through), so it can sit inside a
`chain` without altering the piped value.

**Scoping** — where a `set` is visible depends on which context object it
runs in:

- **Descendant scopes**: visible in any context derived *after* the `set`
  (child scopes resolve ancestor variables through the parent chain; the first
  `set` in a child materializes inherited variables for write isolation).
- **Later siblings, same scope**: when a `set` runs directly at a literal-dict
  key or list element, later-evaluated siblings in that dict/list share the
  same context and can `get` the variable. Earlier siblings cannot — visibility
  follows dict key / list index order.
- **First `chain` func**: runs in the caller's context, so a `set` there is
  visible to later `chain` funcs and to sibling templates outside the `chain`.
- **Later `chain` funcs, `map`/`filter` items, etc.**: run in derived contexts;
  their `set` values stay inside that scope and do not escape to parents or
  already-evaluated siblings.

`include` starts a separate transformation — variables do not cross that
boundary.

examples.doc:
Gets a `list` of values discovered in nested input structure of `dicts` with paths defined in input data.
List of paths may include any number of paths of any depth.
examples.name: AttrDynamicReferenceMultipleNames

examples.doc:
Sets some names to context, one with `true` value and one with `false`.
Iterates over input `list` of string and filters out items with name that is not set to `true` in the context.
examples.name: FilterListNoContent

examples.doc:
A variable stored at one literal-dict key is visible to later sibling keys
evaluated in the same scope (dict insertion order).
examples.name: SetVisibleToLaterSiblingKey

examples.doc:
A variable stored at one literal-dict key is not visible to sibling keys
that were already evaluated — visibility depends on dict key order.
examples.name: SetInvisibleToEarlierSiblingKey

examples.doc:
The first func of a `chain` runs in the caller's context, so a `set` there
is visible to later sibling templates outside the `chain`.
examples.name: SetInFirstChainFuncVisibleToSibling

examples.doc:
A `set` in a non-first `chain` func writes to a derived context and is not
visible to sibling templates outside the `chain`.
examples.name: SetInLaterChainFuncNotVisibleToSibling

examples.doc:
A `set` in the first `chain` func is copied into derived contexts and is
visible to later funcs in the same `chain`.
examples.name: SetInFirstChainFuncVisibleToLaterFunc

examples.doc:
A variable stored before a `map` is copied into each iteration context and
is visible inside the loop.
examples.name: SetBeforeMapVisibleInsideIteration

examples.doc:
A variable stored inside a `map` iteration is not visible after the loop
ends — each iteration uses a derived context.
examples.name: SetInsideMapNotVisibleAfterLoop

rules.name: set

params.description: Name of the variable. Can be dynamic. The names `this`, `item`, `key`, `value` and `index` are reserved and cannot be used as variable names. Raises `TransformationError` if the name evaluates to `NO_CONTENT`. See the rule docstring for scoping rules.

examples.doc:
A variable stored at one literal-dict key is visible to later sibling keys
evaluated in the same scope (dict insertion order).
examples.name: SetVisibleToLaterSiblingKey

params.name: name

rules.description:
Returns the value stored under the given name in the *current* context.

Ancestor variables are available because `derive()` links child scopes to their
parent and `get` walks that chain — but only variables that were already `set`
before that derivation. A `set` in a sibling template or a later `chain` func that
shares no context object with this evaluation will not be found.

Returns `NO_CONTENT` when the name is undefined, unless `default` is
provided.

examples.doc:
When a dynamic attribute name evaluates to no value, lookup returns no value
uniformly — including on lists, where an invalid index type would otherwise fail.
examples.name: AttrDynamicNameNoContent

examples.doc:
Gets a `list` of values discovered in nested input structure of `dicts` with paths defined in input data.
List of paths may include any number of paths of any depth.
examples.name: AttrDynamicReferenceMultipleNames

examples.doc: Returns a fallback number when an undefined variable is requested.
examples.name: GetWithDefault

examples.doc:
Sets some names to context, one with `true` value and one with `false`.
Iterates over input `list` of string and filters out items with name that is not set to `true` in the context.
examples.name: FilterListNoContent

examples.doc:

This results into no value which translates into `None`.

examples.doc:
Creates a dict with item key that has no value.

examples.doc:
Creates a dict with item value that has no value.
It results with empty object.

examples.doc:
Builds a multi-key object in `fields` mode where one value has no value.
The entry whose value resolves to no value is omitted from the result.
examples.name: NoContentForObjectFields

examples.doc: Demonstrates skipping of items in `list` generation by `map` rule when they have no value.
examples.name: NoContentForMappingList

examples.doc: Demonstrates skipping of items in `dict` generation by `map` rule when they have no value.
examples.name: NoContentForMappingDict

examples.doc:
A variable stored at one literal-dict key is visible to later sibling keys
evaluated in the same scope (dict insertion order).
examples.name: SetVisibleToLaterSiblingKey

examples.doc:
A variable stored at one literal-dict key is not visible to sibling keys
that were already evaluated — visibility depends on dict key order.
examples.name: SetInvisibleToEarlierSiblingKey

examples.doc:
The first func of a `chain` runs in the caller's context, so a `set` there
is visible to later sibling templates outside the `chain`.
examples.name: SetInFirstChainFuncVisibleToSibling

examples.doc:
A `set` in a non-first `chain` func writes to a derived context and is not
visible to sibling templates outside the `chain`.
examples.name: SetInLaterChainFuncNotVisibleToSibling

examples.doc:
A `set` in the first `chain` func is copied into derived contexts and is

examples.name: SetInFirstChainFuncVisibleToLaterFunc

examples.doc:
A variable stored before a `map` is copied into each iteration context and
is visible inside the loop.
examples.name: SetBeforeMapVisibleInsideIteration

examples.doc:
A variable stored inside a `map` iteration is not visible after the loop
ends — each iteration uses a derived context.

rules.name: get

params.description: Name of the variable. Can be dynamic. The names `this`, `item`, `key`, `value` and `index` are reserved and cannot be used as variable names. Raises `TransformationError` if the name evaluates to `NO_CONTENT`. See the rule docstring for scoping rules.

examples.doc:
A variable stored at one literal-dict key is visible to later sibling keys
evaluated in the same scope (dict insertion order).

params.name: name

params.description: Optional template for value returned when the variable is undefined. Can be dynamic.

examples.doc: Returns a fallback number when an undefined variable is requested.

params.name: default

rules.description:
Returns values of attribute or item from current value in context.
Can search in deeply nested structures with path.
If attribute is not present returns no value.
If the dynamic name or any path segment evaluates to `NO_CONTENT`, returns
no value (uniformly for all container types).

Parameters are mutually exclusive.

examples.doc: Returns a fallback string when a missing attribute is looked up.
examples.name: AttrWithDefault

examples.doc: `or` treats `NO_CONTENT` as falsy, so a missing value falls through to the fallback.
examples.name: ExprOrNoContentFallback

examples.doc: Formats a dict using a pattern string taken from the same input.
examples.name: FormatWithDynamicPattern

examples.doc:
Produces no result when a formatted value is missing instead of interpolating
the sentinel repr into the output string.
examples.name: FormatSkipsMissingValue

examples.doc:
Returns no result when a named attribute is absent from the input.
Used as an included sub-template in ``IncludeWithDefault``.
examples.name: LookupMissingAttr

examples.doc: Joins two strings using a separator taken from input data.
examples.name: JoinTwoStringsWithDynamicSep

examples.doc: Joins optional string fields, omitting any that are missing.
examples.name: JoinSkipsMissingStringFields

examples.doc:
Dispatches on a value read from the input and emits the matching case. Only the
selected case template is evaluated — the other arms are never walked — so
`switch` is the lazy alternative to building a dict and indexing it.
examples.name: SwitchDispatchOnKey

params.description:
Name of attribute to search. 
It is possible to use a number as the name for items in arrays. 
Can be dynamic.

When a dynamic attribute name evaluates to no value, lookup returns no value

examples.doc:
Builds a single-item dict whose attribute name comes from the input.

examples.name: ObjectDynamicKeyValue

examples.doc:
Produces an object whose key is the marker (`$`) itself.

In `fields` mode the keys of the mapping are emitted verbatim, so `$` is kept
as literal data instead of being interpreted as a rule invocation. The values
are still walked as templates, so the emitted document can mix literal marker
keys with dynamic values — here transon is used to generate a transon template.
examples.name: ObjectFieldsLiteralMarker

examples.doc:
Builds a multi-key object with literal keys and dynamic values.

This is handy for documents that use `$`-prefixed operator keys (e.g. MongoDB
queries): the keys stay literal while each value is interpolated from input.
examples.name: ObjectFieldsMultipleLiteralKeys

examples.doc:
Dispatches on a value read from the input and emits the matching case. Only the
selected case template is evaluated — the other arms are never walked — so
`switch` is the lazy alternative to building a dict and indexing it.
examples.name: SwitchDispatchOnKey

params.name: name

params.description:
List of attribute names or indexes to search in nested structure. 
Can be dynamic.

examples.doc: Gets a `list` of values with 4 elements discovered in nested input structure of `dicts` with paths defined in template.
examples.name: AttrSimpleFixedNames

examples.doc: Gets value from nested input structure of `dicts` with paths defined in different attribute of input data.
examples.name: AttrDynamicReferenceNames

List of paths may include any number of paths of any depth.

params.name: names

params.description: Optional template for value returned when the attribute or path is missing. Can be dynamic.

examples.doc: Returns a fallback string when a missing attribute is looked up.
examples.name: AttrWithDefault

params.name: default

rules.description:
Returns a dict.

In the default `key`/`value` mode it returns a dict with a single,
dynamically named item; this is useful when the attribute name itself comes
from the input. If you need multiple attributes use `join` or `map`. If `key`
or `value` returns no result then the rule returns an empty dict.

In the `fields` mode it builds a dict from a literal mapping: each key is
emitted verbatim and each value is walked as a template. Because the keys are
literal, this is the canonical way to produce an object whose key is the
marker (`$`) itself — data that would otherwise be interpreted as a rule
invocation. Values that evaluate to `NO_CONTENT` are skipped, omitting the
corresponding key from the result.

examples.doc:
Iterates over input list twice.
First time produces list of `dicts` with keys and values defined in separate attributes of input items.
Second time produces list of `dicts` but with keys and values swapped.
Joins together results of two iteration into single list.
Then joins together items of the list into single resulting object.
examples.name: JoinManyDynamicDicts

examples.doc:
Builds a single-item dict whose attribute name comes from the input.
Both the key and the value are dynamic templates.
examples.name: ObjectDynamicKeyValue

examples.doc:
Produces an object whose key is the marker (`$`) itself.

In `fields` mode the keys of the mapping are emitted verbatim, so `$` is kept
as literal data instead of being interpreted as a rule invocation. The values
are still walked as templates, so the emitted document can mix literal marker
keys with dynamic values — here transon is used to generate a transon template.
examples.name: ObjectFieldsLiteralMarker

rules.name: object

params.description: Defines template for name of attribute. Mutually exclusive with `fields`.

Creates a dict with item key that has no value.

examples.doc:
Builds a single-item dict whose attribute name comes from the input.

params.name: key

params.description: Defines template for value of attribute. Mutually exclusive with `fields`.

examples.doc:
Builds a single-item dict whose attribute name comes from the input.

examples.name: ObjectDynamicKeyValue

params.name: value

params.description: A literal mapping whose keys are emitted verbatim — including the marker (`$`), so this is how you produce an object key that is the marker itself (data that would otherwise be read as a rule invocation). Each value is walked as a template. Entries whose value evaluates to `NO_CONTENT` are omitted. Mutually exclusive with `key`/`value`.

examples.doc:
Produces an object whose key is the marker (`$`) itself.

In `fields` mode the keys of the mapping are emitted verbatim, so `$` is kept
as literal data instead of being interpreted as a rule invocation. The values
are still walked as templates, so the emitted document can mix literal marker
keys with dynamic values — here transon is used to generate a transon template.
examples.name: ObjectFieldsLiteralMarker

examples.doc:
Builds a multi-key object with literal keys and dynamic values.

This is handy for documents that use `$`-prefixed operator keys (e.g. MongoDB
queries): the keys stay literal while each value is interpolated from input.
examples.name: ObjectFieldsMultipleLiteralKeys

rules.description:
Transposes iterables like Python's `zip`: rows become columns and columns become rows.
Each output row is a **list** (JSON-friendly), not a Python tuple.

examples.doc:
Zips two lists containing keys and values together into pairs.
Then iterates over list of pairs and produces resulting dict.
examples.name: MapListsToDict

examples.doc:
Transposes parallel lists into rows of paired values.
Each row is a list (not a Python tuple), so the result is JSON-friendly.
examples.name: ZipLists

examples.doc: Strings are iterables: zipping them pairs characters, like Python's `zip`.
examples.name: ZipStrings

rules.name: zip

params.description: Defines the list of lists

examples.doc:
Transposes parallel lists into rows of paired values.
Each row is a list (not a Python tuple), so the result is JSON-friendly.
examples.name: ZipLists

rules.description:
Writes a file using `write_file` delegate (a parameter to `Transformer` constructor).
This rule produces no result.
File will not be written if `name` or `content` returns no result.

examples.doc:
The ``file`` rule writes output through a ``file_writer`` delegate passed to
``Transformer(...)``. It always returns ``NO_CONTENT`` (shown as ``null`` in JSON),
so ``map`` over it yields an empty list — the side effect is the write, not the
transform result. ``name`` and ``content`` are templates; if either evaluates to
``NO_CONTENT``, nothing is written for that invocation.
examples.name: FileWriteViaMap

rules.name: file

params.description: Defines template for file name

examples.doc:
The ``file`` rule writes output through a ``file_writer`` delegate passed to
``Transformer(...)``. It always returns ``NO_CONTENT`` (shown as ``null`` in JSON),
so ``map`` over it yields an empty list — the side effect is the write, not the
transform result. ``name`` and ``content`` are templates; if either evaluates to
``NO_CONTENT``, nothing is written for that invocation.
examples.name: FileWriteViaMap

params.name: name

params.description: Defines template for file content. File content always has JSON formatted data

examples.doc:
The ``file`` rule writes output through a ``file_writer`` delegate passed to
``Transformer(...)``. It always returns ``NO_CONTENT`` (shown as ``null`` in JSON),
so ``map`` over it yields an empty list — the side effect is the write, not the
transform result. ``name`` and ``content`` are templates; if either evaluates to
``NO_CONTENT``, nothing is written for that invocation.
examples.name: FileWriteViaMap

rules.description:
Joins (concatenates) together several dicts, lists of strings.
If items to be concatenated have different types an exception will be thrown.
Items that evaluate to `NO_CONTENT` are omitted before concatenation.
When no items remain, returns `NO_CONTENT` unless `default` is provided.

examples.doc: Enriches input dict with default value.
examples.name: JoinWithStaticNoOverrides

examples.doc: Enriches input dict with default value.
examples.name: JoinWithStaticWithOverrides

examples.doc: Joins two dicts taken from separate attributes of input into one dict.
examples.name: JoinTwoDictsNoCommonKeys

examples.doc: Joins two dicts taken from separate attributes of input into one dict.
examples.name: JoinTwoDictsWithCommonKeys

examples.doc: Joins two dicts taken from separate attributes of input into one dict.
examples.name: JoinTwoStrings

examples.doc: Joins two strings using a separator taken from input data.
examples.name: JoinTwoStringsWithDynamicSep

Iterates over input list twice.
First time produces list of `dicts` with keys and values defined in separate attributes of input items.
Second time produces list of `dicts` but with keys and values swapped.
Joins together results of two iteration into single list.
Then joins together items of the list into single resulting object.

examples.doc: Joins optional string fields, omitting any that are missing.
examples.name: JoinSkipsMissingStringFields

examples.doc: Joining an empty list of items produces no value (`NO_CONTENT`).

examples.name: JoinEmptyWithDefaultDict

examples.doc:
Maps two lists obtained from different attributes of input.
Then joins two obtained lists into single resulting list.
examples.name: MapListToList

rules.name: join

examples.doc: Joins two dicts taken from separate attributes of input into one dict.

params.description: Defines separator for strings concatenation. Can be dynamic. Used only when all items are strings; must evaluate to a string (raises `TransformationError` otherwise). Defaults to empty string.

examples.name: JoinTwoStringsWithDynamicSep

params.name: default

rules.description:
Function composition operation.
Build function composition and executes it against value in current context.
`chain(f1, f2, f3)(x) === f3(f2(f1(x)))`

Each function execution creates new context that will be passed to next function.
The **first** func runs in the caller's context (so a `set` there is visible
outside the `chain`); each subsequent func runs in a derived context.
```plantuml
@startuml
left to right direction
rectangle this
rectangle "intermediate context" as i1
rectangle "intermediate context" as i2
rectangle result
this --> i1: f1
i1 --> i2: f2
i2 --> result: f3
@enduml
```

examples.doc:
Gets a `list` of values discovered in nested input structure of `dicts` with paths defined in input data.
List of paths may include any number of paths of any depth.
examples.name: AttrDynamicReferenceMultipleNames

examples.doc: Searching for values in nested structure with sequence of `attr` operations chained together.
examples.name: ChainWithAttr

examples.doc:
Adds prefix to input string.
Illustrates functionality of `expr` rule in form of monad and usage of `parent` rule.
examples.name: ExprSimpleMonads2

examples.doc:
For input dictionary containing two integers returns sum of squares.
Illustrates functionality of `expr` rule in form of monad.
examples.name: ExprMonadsComplex

examples.doc:
Sets some names to context, one with `true` value and one with `false`.
Iterates over input `list` of string and filters out items with name that is not set to `true` in the context.
examples.name: FilterListNoContent

examples.doc:
Maps two lists obtained from different attributes of input.
Then joins two obtained lists into single resulting list.
examples.name: MapListToList

examples.doc:
Zips two lists containing keys and values together into pairs.
Then iterates over list of pairs and produces resulting dict.
examples.name: MapListsToDict

examples.doc:
Gets variable names `foo` that does not exist from context.
Then tries to get attribute named `bar` of non-existing value.
This results into no value which translates into `None`.
examples.name: NoContentForAttr

examples.doc:
The first func of a `chain` runs in the caller's context, so a `set` there
is visible to later sibling templates outside the `chain`.
examples.name: SetInFirstChainFuncVisibleToSibling

examples.doc:
A `set` in a non-first `chain` func writes to a derived context and is not
visible to sibling templates outside the `chain`.
examples.name: SetInLaterChainFuncNotVisibleToSibling

examples.doc:
A `set` in the first `chain` func is copied into derived contexts and is
visible to later funcs in the same `chain`.
examples.name: SetInFirstChainFuncVisibleToLaterFunc

examples.doc:
A variable stored before a `map` is copied into each iteration context and
is visible inside the loop.
examples.name: SetBeforeMapVisibleInsideIteration

examples.doc:
A variable stored inside a `map` iteration is not visible after the loop
ends — each iteration uses a derived context.
examples.name: SetInsideMapNotVisibleAfterLoop

rules.name: chain

params.description: Define list of templates (not just rules) for chaining. The list structure is constant; each element is walked as a template.

examples.doc: Searching for values in nested structure with sequence of `attr` operations chained together.
examples.name: ChainWithAttr

params.name: funcs

rules.description:
Executes unary or binary operation.
There are 3 modes of running this rule:

1. **No attributes were specified:**
Result is unary operation calculation against current context value.
2. **Single `value` was specified:**
Result is binary operation calculation where the
first argument is current values of context and
the second is specified value.
3. **Multiple `values` were specified:**
Result is calculated by applying operation to all values in pairs (reduction).
In this case current context value is ignored.

Parameters are mutually exclusive.

examples.doc:
Evaluates each arm's `when` in order and emits the `then` of the first truthy
one. Only the selected `then` is walked; later arms are skipped. This is the
ordered, multi-way generalization of an `if`/`else` chain.
examples.name: CondFirstTruthyArm

examples.doc: Falls back to the `default` template when no arm's `when` is truthy.
examples.name: CondDefaultBranch

examples.doc: Iterates over input `dict` and filters out items with even value.
examples.name: FilterDict

examples.doc: Iterates over input `list` and filters out items with even value.
examples.name: FilterList

examples.doc:
Filters a list, keeping only elements at odd indices (1, 3, 5, ...).
Demonstrates that ``index`` works inside ``filter`` as well as ``map``.
examples.name: FilterListByOddIndex

rules.name: expr

params.description:
Defines name of operation. This is always constant.

###### Possible values:

| operator     | alternative | kind   | types          | result         |
|--------------|-------------|--------|----------------|----------------|
| <            | lt          | binary | any            | boolean        |
| <=           | le          | binary | any            | boolean        |
| ==           | eq          | binary | any            | boolean        |
| !=           | ne          | binary | any            | boolean        |
| >=           | ge          | binary | any            | boolean        |
| >         | gt          | binary | any            | boolean        |
| +            | add         | binary | string, number | string, number |
| -            | sub         | binary | number         | number         |
| *            | mul         | binary | number         | number         |
| /            | div         | binary | number         | number         |
| %            | mod         | binary | number         | number         |
| &&           | and         | binary | any            | any            |
| || | or          | binary | any            | any            |
| !            | not         | unary  | boolean        | boolean        |

You can use code-style or mnemonic name (i.e. operator or alternative).

examples.doc:
`and`/`&&` use Python logical conjunction (truthiness), not bitwise `&`.
With integers, `6 and 3` yields `3`, not `2`.
examples.name: ExprLogicalAnd

examples.doc:
`or`/`||` use Python logical disjunction (truthiness), not bitwise `|`.
Returns the first truthy operand or the last operand.
examples.name: ExprLogicalOr

examples.doc: `or` treats `NO_CONTENT` as falsy, so a missing value falls through to the fallback.
examples.name: ExprOrNoContentFallback

examples.doc: Calculates negation of input using `expr` rule with unary operator as monad.
examples.name: ExprUnary1

examples.doc: Calculates negation of input using `expr` rule with unary operator in alternative form.
examples.name: ExprUnary2

params.name: op

params.description: Defines template for single parameter value

examples.doc:
Evaluates each arm's `when` in order and emits the `then` of the first truthy
one. Only the selected `then` is walked; later arms are skipped. This is the
ordered, multi-way generalization of an `if`/`else` chain.
examples.name: CondFirstTruthyArm

Adds 100 to input integer and converts result into string.
Illustrates usage of `call` attribute `value`.

examples.doc:
Adds suffix to input string.
Illustrates functionality of `expr` rule in form of monad.
examples.name: ExprSimpleMonads1

examples.doc:
Adds prefix to input string.
Illustrates functionality of `expr` rule in form of monad and usage of `parent` rule.
examples.name: ExprSimpleMonads2

examples.doc:
For input dictionary containing two integers returns sum of squares.
Illustrates functionality of `expr` rule in form of monad.
examples.name: ExprMonadsComplex

examples.doc:
`<`/`lt` returns `true` when the current context value is strictly less than the
given value.
examples.name: OperatorLessThan

examples.doc:
`<=`/`le` returns `true` when the current context value is less than or equal to the
given value.
examples.name: OperatorLessOrEqual

examples.doc: `==`/`eq` returns `true` when both operands are equal.
examples.name: OperatorEqual

examples.doc: `!=`/`ne` returns `true` when the operands differ.
examples.name: OperatorNotEqual

examples.doc:
`>=`/`ge` returns `true` when the current context value is greater than or equal to
the given value.
examples.name: OperatorGreaterOrEqual

examples.doc:
`>`/`gt` returns `true` when the current context value is strictly greater than the
given value.
examples.name: OperatorGreaterThan

examples.doc: `-`/`sub` subtracts the given value from the current context value.
examples.name: OperatorSubtract

examples.doc: `*`/`mul` multiplies the current context value by the given value.
examples.name: OperatorMultiply

examples.doc: `/`/`div` performs true division; the result is a float.
examples.name: OperatorDivide

examples.doc:
`%`/`mod` returns the remainder of dividing the current context value by the given
value.
examples.name: OperatorModulo

params.name: value

params.description:
Defines template for multiple parameter values.

> **Warning:** Unlike unary mode and `value` mode, **`this` is not used** when `values`
> is specified. The operator is applied via `reduce(op, values)` over the evaluated
> list only. Include `{"$": "this"}` as a list item if you need the current context
> value in the reduction.

examples.doc:
Adds suffix to input string.
Illustrates functionality of `expr` rule with direct parameters.
examples.name: ExprSimpleValues1

examples.doc:
Adds prefix to input string.
Illustrates functionality of `expr` rule with direct parameters.
examples.name: ExprSimpleValues2

examples.doc:
For input dictionary containing two integers returns sum of squares.
Illustrates functionality of `expr` rule with direct parameters.
examples.name: ExprValuesComplex

examples.doc:
With `values`, the operator runs as `reduce(op, evaluated_list)` — the current
context value is not an implicit argument. Input data affects the result only
when you reference it explicitly inside a list item (e.g. `{"$": "this"}`).
examples.name: ExprValuesIgnoresThis

examples.doc:
Iterates over list of values.
For formatting value creates dict with dynamically calculated values.
Produces list of strings using formatting.
examples.name: FormatWithValue

params.name: values

rules.description:
Runs function registered in `Transformer`.
Usually, that is used to convert values into different type, but you can also use it to call any function.

There are 3 modes of running this rule:

1. **No attributes were specified:**
Runs conversion function with single parameter - value of current context.
2. **Single `value` was specified:**
Runs conversion function with single parameter - provided value.
Current context is ignored in this case.
3. **Multiple `values` were specified:**
Runs conversion function with multiple parameters.
Current context is ignored in this case.

Parameters are mutually exclusive.

examples.doc: Converts input integer into string.
examples.name: ConvertNoParameters

examples.doc: Parses an input string into a floating-point number using the `float` function.
examples.name: ConvertToFloat

rules.name: call

params.description:
Defines the name of function. This is always constant.

###### Possible values:

| name  | input | output |
|-------|:-----:|:------:|
| str   |  any  |  str   | 
| int   |  str  |  int   | 
| float |  str  | float  |

examples.doc: Converts input integer into string.
examples.name: ConvertNoParameters

params.name: name

params.description: Defines template for single parameter value

Adds 100 to input integer and converts result into string.
Illustrates usage of `call` attribute `value`.

params.description: Defines template for multiple parameters values

examples.doc: Converts input string into integer in base-16.

params.name: values

rules.description:
Execute string formatting using current value.

If `value` is specified it is used for formatting otherwise current context value is used.

See [this page](https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/inputoutput.html#the-string-format-method)
for examples of how formatting in python works.

If value for formatting is `dict` it will be unpacked to separate names.
If value is list if will be unpacked to separate items.
Otherwise while value will be passed to formatting.

Returns no value when the formatting value (or any unpacked list element or dict
key/value) is `NO_CONTENT`, unless `default` is provided.

examples.doc: Returns a fallback label when a formatted value is missing.
examples.name: FormatWithDefault

examples.doc: Iterates list with floats and produces list of strings by formatting each item.
examples.name: FormatFromSingleValue

examples.doc: Iterates over list of dicts and produces list of string using items for filling pattern slots.
examples.name: FormatFromDict

examples.doc: Iterates over list of lists and produces list of string using items for filling pattern slots.
examples.name: FormatFromList

examples.doc: Formats a dict using a pattern string taken from the same input.
examples.name: FormatWithDynamicPattern

examples.doc:
Produces no result when a formatted value is missing instead of interpolating
the sentinel repr into the output string.
examples.name: FormatSkipsMissingValue

rules.name: format

params.description: Defines pattern for string formatting. Can be dynamic. Must evaluate to a string (raises `TransformationError` otherwise).

examples.doc: Formats a dict using a pattern string taken from the same input.
examples.name: FormatWithDynamicPattern

params.name: pattern

params.description: Defines template for input data. Optional.

examples.doc:
Iterates over list of values.
For formatting value creates dict with dynamically calculated values.
Produces list of strings using formatting.
examples.name: FormatWithValue

examples.doc:
Produces no result when a formatted value is missing instead of interpolating
the sentinel repr into the output string.
examples.name: FormatSkipsMissingValue

params.name: value

params.description: Optional template for value returned when the formatting value (or any unpacked list element or dict key/value) is `NO_CONTENT`. Can be dynamic.

examples.doc: Returns a fallback label when a formatted value is missing.
examples.name: FormatWithDefault

params.name: default

rules.description:
Loads and runs another template using the `template_loader` delegate
(a parameter to `Transformer` constructor).

Only the current context value is passed to the included template — variables
stored with `set`/`get` and iteration accessors do not cross the boundary.

When the loaded sub-template still uses the default marker (`$`), it inherits
the **parent transformer's marker** so staged templates stay consistent across
`include` boundaries. A sub-template that pins a non-default marker keeps it.

Nested includes are limited by `max_include_depth` on `Transformer` (default 50).
Exceeding the limit raises `TransformationError` with the include name chain.

If the included template produces no result, this rule produces no result unless
`default` is provided.

examples.doc: Returns a fallback when the included template produces no result.
examples.name: IncludeWithDefault

examples.doc:
Illustrates usage of `include` rule by iterating over input `list` and applying different template to each item.
Produces a `list` of results applying `MapListsToDict` template.
For all examples in this documentation you can use them under their name in include command.
This was achieved by providing special `template_loader` factory to `Transformer` class.
examples.name: Include

rules.name: include

params.description: Name or path to template. Can be dynamic. Meaning of this `name` depends on provided template loader.

examples.doc:
Illustrates usage of `include` rule by iterating over input `list` and applying different template to each item.
Produces a `list` of results applying `MapListsToDict` template.
For all examples in this documentation you can use them under their name in include command.
This was achieved by providing special `template_loader` factory to `Transformer` class.
examples.name: Include

params.name: name

params.description: Optional template for value returned when the included template produces no result. Can be dynamic.

examples.doc: Returns a fallback when the included template produces no result.
examples.name: IncludeWithDefault

params.name: default

rules.description:
Lazy equality dispatch: evaluates `key`, then evaluates **only** the matching
entry of `cases`. Non-selected case templates are never walked, so `switch`
differs from building a dict and indexing it (which would evaluate every arm).

Matching is by equality between the evaluated `key` and the literal case keys.
When no case matches — including when `key` evaluates to `NO_CONTENT` — the
`default` template is evaluated if present, otherwise the rule produces no
value.

examples.doc:
Dispatches on a value read from the input and emits the matching case. Only the
selected case template is evaluated — the other arms are never walked — so
`switch` is the lazy alternative to building a dict and indexing it.
examples.name: SwitchDispatchOnKey

examples.doc:
Falls back to the `default` template when no case matches the dispatch key
(this also covers the case where the key evaluates to no value).
examples.name: SwitchDefaultBranch

rules.name: switch

params.description: Template for the value to dispatch on. Can be dynamic. Compared by equality against the (literal) keys of `cases`.

examples.doc:
Dispatches on a value read from the input and emits the matching case. Only the
selected case template is evaluated — the other arms are never walked — so
`switch` is the lazy alternative to building a dict and indexing it.
examples.name: SwitchDispatchOnKey

params.name: key

params.description: A mapping from case value to result template. Only the template of the selected case is evaluated; the others are never walked.

examples.doc:
Dispatches on a value read from the input and emits the matching case. Only the
selected case template is evaluated — the other arms are never walked — so
`switch` is the lazy alternative to building a dict and indexing it.
examples.name: SwitchDispatchOnKey

params.name: cases

params.description: Optional template evaluated when no case matches (including when `key` evaluates to `NO_CONTENT`). Can be dynamic.

examples.doc:
Falls back to the `default` template when no case matches the dispatch key
(this also covers the case where the key evaluates to no value).
examples.name: SwitchDefaultBranch

params.name: default

rules.description:
Lisp-style ordered multi-way conditional (subsumes a chained `if`/`else`).
Walks each arm's `when` in order; the **first** truthy `when` selects its
`then`, which is the only `then` evaluated. A `when` that evaluates to
`NO_CONTENT` is treated as falsy. When no arm matches, the `default` template
is evaluated if present, otherwise the rule produces no value.

examples.doc:
Evaluates each arm's `when` in order and emits the `then` of the first truthy
one. Only the selected `then` is walked; later arms are skipped. This is the
ordered, multi-way generalization of an `if`/`else` chain.
examples.name: CondFirstTruthyArm

examples.doc: Falls back to the `default` template when no arm's `when` is truthy.
examples.name: CondDefaultBranch

rules.name: cond

params.description: Ordered list of `{when, then}` arms. The first arm whose `when` is truthy selects its `then`; later arms and non-selected `then` templates are never walked.

examples.doc:
Evaluates each arm's `when` in order and emits the `then` of the first truthy
one. Only the selected `then` is walked; later arms are skipped. This is the
ordered, multi-way generalization of an `if`/`else` chain.
examples.name: CondFirstTruthyArm

params.name: cases

params.description: Optional template evaluated when no arm matches. Can be dynamic.

examples.doc:
Evaluates each arm's `when` in order and emits the `then` of the first truthy
one. Only the selected `then` is walked; later arms are skipped. This is the
ordered, multi-way generalization of an `if`/`else` chain.
examples.name: CondFirstTruthyArm

examples.doc: Falls back to the `default` template when no arm's `when` is truthy.
examples.name: CondDefaultBranch

params.name: default
